Does reference-slice conditioning fix the flat-EF amplitude regression?

3-way early read (epoch ~20/200) · reference / diffusion / bspline · held-out val (N=30) · vs prior target_t-index baseline + oracle ceiling

TL;DR & takeaway. Replacing the content-free target_t index with a real target-phase reference slice (VGGT's native camera-token anchor) breaks the flat-EF regression. Predicted ejection fraction, which used to be constant across patients (baseline Pearson r −0.02, slope −0.03), now tracks each patient's true EF: reference r +0.87, slope +0.36 — at only ~10% of training. All three variants recover amplitude. Among them, diffusion (L2 grad-penalty) gives the cleanest per-patient ordering (highest slope +0.71 and r +0.87), while bspline best defeats the renderer confound — its smooth warp yields a sharper V_canon, so absolute predicted EF (47%) is far closer to GT (~63%) than the DPT heads (~11%). The r is the trustworthy ordering signal; the absolute slope stays below 1 mostly because the differentiable splat blurs V_canon and compresses the EF swing (a known renderer confound, docs/10), not a model failure. ES-phase timing stays accurate (≈90–93% within ±1 frame). Verdict: flat-EF is fixed by every variant; final warp/reg choice should be made on the converged run.
Read this before the numbers. (1) These checkpoints are at epoch ~20/200 — directional, not final. (2) Absolute predicted EF is compressed (~11% vs ~63% GT) by splat-blur; the analysis script flags EF as "splat-confounded". So compare models on r and on per-patient curve shape, and read the slope as a lower bound. (3) These are observed-phase reconstructions (slot 0 sees the target phase) — the new contract; PSNR is NOT comparable to the old index runs.

Headline table

modelEF slopeEF Pearson rES corrES within±1curve corr
target_t baseline (docs/24)-0.03-0.02———
reference — DPT head · L1+TV+0.36+0.87+0.7590%0.95
diffusion — DPT head · L2 ‖∇u‖²+0.71+0.87+0.7490%0.96
bspline — B-spline warp head (g=32)+0.65+0.73-0.0593%0.97
oracle splat (docs/24)+1.03+1.00———

Baseline & oracle from docs/24 (the flat-EF proof: index model slope −0.03; oracle-splat of true slices slope 1.03 → the architecture can reach 1.0 when it observes the target phase).

1 · Per-patient EF: predicted vs true

Each dot is one held-out subject; axes fixed 0–100% so the shape of the cloud is honest. The leftmost panel is the prior target_t index model — a horizontal cloud at ~48%: every patient predicted the same EF regardless of truth (r −0.02 = the flat-EF failure). The three reference models show positively-sloped, tight clouds: predicted EF rises with the patient's true EF (r +0.73…+0.87) — the model reads per-patient amplitude from the reference slice. (The clouds sit below the identity line because the splat renderer compresses absolute EF; the slope/correlation, not the absolute height, is what shows the fix.)

2 · Correlation & slope vs baseline and oracle

3 · LV-volume across the cardiac cycle (example patients)

These curves are the nnU-Net LV blood-pool volumes (the data behind the EF numbers) — not the image filmstrips in §5. Shown in absolute mL, low→high true-EF patients left→right. Two distinct things to read here:

  • Timing — every model dips at the correct phase; shape corr ≈ 0.95.
  • Amplitude — the DPT heads (reference, diffusion) render shallow dips and bspline a deeper one. This shallow-vs-deep is RENDERING (splat-blur), not model quality: the smooth bspline warp yields a sharper V_canon so the measured swing is larger. Critically, the dip depth scales with the patient's true EF (deeper as you move right) — that scaling is the fix.

Why "shallow" ≠ "broken": the old target_t baseline actually rendered a deep ~48% swing — but the same depth for every patient (it had no per-patient information). The reference DPT heads render shallower swings that correctly track each patient (the §1 slope). Depth alone is the wrong axis to judge the fix; per-patient ordering (§1) is the right one.

4 · End-systole timing

Which phase contracts most. Timing was never the failure (the old index got it right too); the reference contract preserves it — all three land ≈90–93% within ±1 frame. Note the ES correlation is a noisy statistic here because GT ES has very low spread (it clusters at t6, std ≈ 0.8): when nearly every patient's ES is the same phase there is little variance to correlate, so a near-zero ES corr alongside a high within-±1 (e.g. bspline) means "times ES correctly", not "fails". Read within ±1 as the robust timing metric.

5 · Example reconstructions (mid-ventricular slice, full cardiac cycle)

Top row = on-disk GT phase (V_gt); bottom row = the model's splatted reconstruction (V_canon) for that target phase. Note the visible LV-cavity area change across the cycle — that swing is the amplitude the EF metric reads (and that splat-blur compresses).

reference — Test_P044

reference — Test_P023

diffusion — Test_P044

diffusion — Test_P023

bspline — Test_P044

bspline — Test_P023

Method

For each held-out val subject we hold the scattered input slices fixed and sweep the target phase t=0..11. In the reference contract, slot 0 is a real phases[t_target] slice at the mid-ventricular plane, marked by VGGT's native camera_token anchor (use_reference_token=True, reference_slot=True); the model reads the target phase from that image, not from a target_t index. Each reconstructed V_canon and the GT V_gt are segmented by nnU-Net Task114 (M&Ms, 2D, nnUNetTrainerV2_MMS); LV blood-pool volume per phase → EF = (max−min)/max. Tools: tools/cmrxrecon_phase_analysis/measure_model_contraction.py → analyze_model_contraction.py (repointed to the reference contract). Baseline/oracle: docs/24.

Generated by _html/build_reference_ef_report.py. All model numbers computed from the analysis JSONs.
